question: Which of the following properties must an equivalence relation satisfy?
explanation: An equivalence relation is defined by three properties: Reflexive (a~a), Symmetric (if a~b then b~a), and Transitive (if a~b and b~c then a~c).

question: Given the set A = {1, 2, 3}, which of the following relations on A is an equivalence relation?
explanation: This relation is reflexive (all (1,1), (2,2), (3,3) are present), symmetric (for every (1,2) there is a (2,1)), and transitive (the only non-trivial pair is (1,2) and (2,1), whose composition (1,1) is present). Option 3 is not transitive (it has (1,2) and (2,3) but not (1,3)). Option 4 is not symmetric (it has (1,2) but no (2,1)) and not transitive (it has (1,2) and (2,3) but no (1,3)).

question: For the equivalence relation R = {(a,a), (b,b), (c,c), (a,b), (b,a)} on the set S = {a, b, c}, what is the equivalence class of element c?
explanation: The equivalence class of an element x is the set of all elements related to x. Since c is only related to itself (the pairs are (c,c)), its equivalence class is {c}. Elements a and b are related to each other, so they form a separate equivalence class {a, b}.

question: What is the partition of the set Z (integers) induced by the equivalence relation 'congruence modulo 5'?
options: Five sets: the sets of integers with remainders 0, 1, 2, 3, and 4 when divided by 5.
Two sets: the even integers and the odd integers.
An infinite number of sets, each containing a single integer.
Five sets: the sets of positive integers, negative integers, zero, even numbers, and odd numbers.
explanation: Congruence modulo 5 is an equivalence relation. Two integers are equivalent if they have the same remainder when divided by 5. The five distinct equivalence classes (which form the partition) are [0]₅, [1]₅, [2]₅, [3]₅, and [4]₅, representing all integers with remainders 0, 1, 2, 3, and 4 respectively.

question: Let R be an equivalence relation on a set A. According to the Fundamental Theorem of Equivalence Relations, what is the direct connection between R and the concept of a partition?
options: The equivalence classes of R form a partition of A.
Every partition of A defines a unique equivalence relation on A.
Both A and B are true.
An equivalence relation is itself a partition of the Cartesian product A × A.
explanation: The Fundamental Theorem of Equivalence Relations states a two-way connection: 1) The equivalence classes of any equivalence relation on A form a partition of A. 2) Conversely, for any partition of A, there is an equivalence relation on A whose equivalence classes are precisely the sets of the partition. Therefore, both statements are true and describe the fundamental connection between the two concepts.
